Commercial Mar 29, 2026

How The Queen Of The Skies Made A Comeback At Delta Air Lines

The story behind the 747's last hurrah in the US.

Delta's 747 retirement signals the end of an era for wide-body quad-jet operations in US commercial aviation as airlines pivot to more fuel-efficient twin-engine aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

Misawa’s 13th Fighter Squadron Receives First F-35s

Misawa Air Base has received the U.S. Air Force’s first F-35A Lightning II fighters to be permanently based in Japan, beginning the transition from the F-16 to the fifth-gen aircraft. Misawa Air Base, home of the U.S. Air Force’s 35th Fighter Wing, has received its first four F-35A Lightning II...

Japan's operational F-35A deployment signals a major shift in Indo-Pacific air defense capabilities and strengthens U.S. forward presence amid regional tensions.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Do US Airlines Still Use The Boeing 757 On Key Routes?

Dozens of 757s still fly to this day, but four decades after the aircraft's introduction, why is this?

The 757's unmatched efficiency on thin transatlantic routes and superior hot-and-high performance remain irreplaceable, making retirement economically unfeasible despite its age.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 29, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern aviation SMS shifts the industry from identifying problems after accidents to systematically preventing them before they occur through data-driven risk assessment.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Military Mar 29, 2026

Tight Space: Inside The B-2 Spirit Cockpit

The B-2 Spirit has to perform extra-long missions, while providing less space and comfort for its pilots than transport aircraft like the C-17.

The B-2's cramped two-pilot cockpit design reflects a deliberate trade-off prioritizing stealth and range over ergonomics, requiring specialized pilot conditioning for 15+ hour missions.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Press Release: Aer Lingus takes off with Starlink inflight Wi-Fi

Aer Lingus has equipped the first aircraft in its fleet with Starlink, delivering ultrafast, reliable Wi-Fi to customers and marking a major step forward for customer experience. Customers travelling from Dublin to New York JFK on flight EI105 today are the first to experience the innovative...

Aer Lingus's Starlink deployment signals a competitive pressure for legacy carriers to modernize connectivity infrastructure, as satellite-based systems bypass traditional air-to-ground networks.

See Runway Girl Network →

Drones Mar 29, 2026

Unidentified Drones Crash Near Kouvola, Finland, Authorities Investigate Possible Origin

Finnish authorities are investigating reports that one or two unmanned aerial systems havecrashed in southeastern...

Finland's drone crash investigation may reveal transnational airspace security gaps as neighboring regions increasingly deploy unidentified systems.

See sUAS News →

PAI
Safety Mar 29, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Effective SMS relies on psychological safety that encourages frontline crews to report hazards without fear of punishment, directly reducing accident precursors.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Op-Ed: Seating architecture must evolve to accommodate power, IFEC

Please enjoy this Op-Ed contribution from Unum VP Business Development, Alan McInnes Moving beyond the traditional considerations of structure, comfort and certification, aircraft seating is a critical integration point for power, in-flight entertainment (IFE) and connectivity systems that...

Modern aircraft seats must serve as integrated power distribution hubs for passenger devices and IFE systems, requiring designers to rethink structural architecture beyond traditional comfort parameters.

See Runway Girl Network →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

The One Metric That Matters Most In Premium Economy

Premium economy has grown significantly in popularity over the past decade.

Premium economy's revenue potential depends heavily on seat density optimization, as airlines balance higher fares against cabin space constraints that limit unit revenue growth.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Southwest Airlines Adds 42 New Nonstop Routes This Spring [Map]

They include four new cities, with one in particular being historic...

Southwest's expansion into four new markets signals capacity deployment in leisure-driven segments where point-to-point service can undercut legacy carrier hubbing economics.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Mar 29, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Regional airports like CID serve dual roles as both flight training hubs and commercial carriers, diversifying revenue streams across general and commercial aviation segments.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Is Airbus Creating A New A220?

Airbus is moving ahead with plans for an updated A220.

Airbus's A220 refresh signals intensified competition in the 130-160 seat segment where Boeing's 737 MAX dominance creates significant market vulnerability.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

6 Aircraft That Represent The Future Of Air Combat

Discover six aircraft redefining modern air warfare, from stealth fighters to missile trucks, shaping the balance of air power in 2026 and beyond.

Next-generation combat aircraft prioritize sensor fusion and autonomous capabilities over raw speed, reflecting a fundamental shift in air warfare doctrine toward information dominance.

See Simple Flying →

Corporate Mar 29, 2026

Former United Boeing 747 Literally Wedged Between Two Seattle Skyscrapers

A decommissioned Boeing 747 will be suspended between two high-rise apartment buildings in downtown Seattle as part of an art project. This cool installation is set to hang around 14 feet (4.2 meters) above the ground between the two towers of the WB 1200 Project, located at 1200 Stewart Street in...

Retired aircraft are increasingly finding second lives in high-profile cultural installations, creating novel marketing and placemaking opportunities for urban real estate developers.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Mar 29, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes remain a leading fatal helicopter hazard despite pilots' overconfidence in reaction time, requiring enhanced detection systems and operational discipline.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Breaking The ICE: Passenger Hands Out Vodka Shots As Federal Agents Man TSA Lines

While ICE agents are handing out water across airports, one passenger at a US airport is pouring out vodka shots for fellow passengers!

Aviation security operations now include federal agents staffing TSA checkpoints, signaling resource constraints that may impact passenger screening throughput and experience.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

India inaugurates Noida International Airport, Delhi’s second gateway

India opened the first phase of Noida International Airport (DXN) in Jewar, Uttar Pradesh, on March 28, 2026,…...

India's new airport capacity addition addresses critical Delhi congestion while positioning the country to capture faster traffic growth than global averages.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Mar 29, 2026

Rare US "Eye In The Sky" Command Plane Destroyed In Iran Strike On Saudi Air Base

The US has just a handful of E-3 Sentry's in mission-capable condition.

Loss of an E-3 Sentry reduces US airborne early warning capacity at a critical moment, as the fleet's aging inventory limits rapid replacement options.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation sectors can simultaneously address capacity constraints and environmental impact, presenting a unified solution to competing industry pressures.

See MDPI →

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF E-3 Sentry AWACS damaged in Iranian strike on Saudi air base

An Iranian missile and drone attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia on March 27, 2026,…...

Loss of AWACS capability in the region could significantly degrade allied air defense coordination across the Middle East, creating operational vulnerabilities for coalition operations.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 29, 2026

‘Shots on goal and win the game’: NASA’s effort to accelerate lunar landings

While NASA outlined plans to increase the cadence of robotic lunar lander missions at a recent event, it said little about accelerating work on crewed lunar landers.

NASA's focus on accelerating robotic lunar missions may indicate resource or technical constraints limiting near-term crewed landing timelines.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF A-10 strike on Iraqi military base kills seven soldiers

An airstrike attributed to a US Air Force A-10 Thunderbolt II hit a military clinic at the Habbaniyah…...

Friendly fire incidents involving legacy platforms like the A-10 underscore the continued operational risks of close air support missions in complex battlefield environments.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Concurrent testing of advanced air-to-air missile systems by competing contractors suggests accelerated development timelines for next-generation air combat capabilities.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's pivot to a new partner signals flexibility in on-orbit servicing partnerships, though delays in demonstration missions could impact the timeline for commercial space infrastructure adoption.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's evaluation of the MQ-28A Ghost Bat signals NATO's shift toward integrating Australian-developed autonomous combat aircraft with European fighter fleets, potentially reshaping allied air superiority strategies.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The F-35C deployment demonstrates carrier-based fifth-generation combat capability integration with amphibious operations during active Middle East escalation.

See The Aviationist →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's pivot to dedicated UAS operations signals traditional rotorcraft manufacturers are establishing independent divisions to compete in the rapidly expanding commercial drone market.

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

Commercial drone manufacturers are pivoting to defense applications, signaling how civilian UAS technology now directly supports national security infrastructure.

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical oil price volatility is forcing governments to recalibrate aviation fuel tax timelines, signaling that sustainable fuel mandates may require price stability mechanisms.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

NASA's accelerated Artemis 2 timeline with less than a week before launch indicates confidence in vehicle readiness and crew preparation despite previous delays.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers reduces available aerial refueling capacity across Middle Eastern operations, directly impacting sustained fighter sortie rates and long-range mission planning.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Deep Space Network expertise from career-long communication specialists like Richards is critical infrastructure enabling human lunar missions beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines accelerates commercial lunar logistics capabilities essential for sustained Artemis operations beyond initial crewed missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework extends aerospace industry accountability beyond flight operations to encompass broader stewardship metrics that could influence contractor selection and funding priorities.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine components from scrapped aircraft may bypass traceability systems, creating hidden safety risks in the supply chain that current documentation practices cannot fully detect.
